news_summary: Scientists are making groundbreaking discoveries about life and nature, from uncovering universal temperature patterns and ancient geological processes to enhancing animal safety and advancing Earth observation through artificial intelligence.
news_summary_fr: Les scientifiques font des découvertes révolutionnaires sur la vie et la nature, qu'il s'agisse de mettre au jour des modèles de température universels et d'anciens processus géologiques, d'améliorer la sécurité des animaux ou de faire progresser l'observation de la Terre grâce à l'intelligence artificielle.
news_summary_es: Los científicos están realizando descubrimientos revolucionarios sobre la vida y la naturaleza, desde el descubrimiento de patrones universales de temperatura y procesos geológicos antiguos hasta la mejora de la seguridad animal y el avance de la observación de la Tierra mediante la inteligencia artificial.

articles.title: Scientists discover a universal temperature curve that governs all life
articles.summary: Researchers have uncovered a universal pattern showing how temperature affects life on Earth. Across thousands of species—from microbes to reptiles—performance rises gradually with warming until an optimal temperature is reached, after which it drops sharply. Although each species has its own preferred temperature range, they all follow the same underlying curve. This surprising constraint suggests evolution may have limited room to help species cope with rapid climate warming.
articles.content:
Researchers at Trinity College Dublin report that they have uncovered what appears to be a "universal thermal performance curve" (UTPC) that applies across the entire tree of life. According to the team, this pattern governs how organisms respond to changes in temperature. The findings suggest that this rule effectively "shackles evolution," because no species studied so far has managed to escape the limits it places on how temperature influences biological performance.

Temperature affects every living organism. The newly described UTPC brings together tens of thousands of previously separate performance curves that scientists have used to understand how well different species function at various temperatures. The researchers found that these curves all follow the same underlying pattern. This applies not only across species, but also across many different types of biological activity. The pattern holds whether scientists are testing how fast lizards run on a treadmill, measuring how sharks swim in the ocean, or tracking how quickly bacterial cells divide.

A Common Pattern in How Life Responds to Heat

The UTPC reveals a consistent relationship between temperature and performance in living organisms. As temperatures rise, biological performance generally improves gradually until reaching an optimal point (where performance is greatest).

Once temperatures move beyond this optimum, however, performance declines rapidly. This steep drop at higher temperatures means that overheating can quickly become dangerous, potentially causing physiological breakdown or even death.

The study, published in the journal PNAS, suggests that species may face stronger limits than previously thought when it comes to adapting to climate change. With temperatures increasing across much of the planet, these constraints could affect how well organisms can cope with future warming.

One Curve Shared Across the Tree of Life

Andrew Jackson, Professor in Zoology in Trinity's School of Natural Sciences and a co author of the study, explained that the research uncovered striking similarities across many forms of life.

"Across thousands of species and almost all groups of life including bacteria, plants, reptiles, fish and insects, the shape of the curve that describes how performance changes with temperature is very similar. However, different species have very different optimal temperatures, ranging from 5°C to 100°C, and their performance can vary a lot depending on the measure of performance being observed and the species in question."

Scientists have previously developed many models to explain the wide range of temperature responses seen in nature. However, the new research indicates that these differences are variations of the same basic curve.

"That has led to countless variations on models being proposed to explain these differences. What we have shown here is that all the different curves are in fact the same exact curve, just stretched and shifted over different temperatures. And what's more, we have shown that the optimal temperature and the critical maximum temperature at which death occurs are inextricably linked."

Jackson also noted that once temperatures rise above the optimum, the range in which organisms can survive becomes narrower.

"Whatever the species, it simply must have a smaller temperature range at which life is viable once temperatures shift above the optimum."

Study Analyzed Thousands of Thermal Performance Curves

Senior author Dr. Nicholas Payne from Trinity's School of Natural Sciences said the conclusions are based on an extensive dataset.

"These results have sprung forward from an in-depth analysis of over 2,500 different thermal performance curves, which comprise a tremendous variety of different performance measures for a similarly tremendous variety of different species -- from bacteria to plants, and from lizards to insects."

The results indicate that this shared pattern appears across nearly every major branch of life that has evolved over billions of years.

"This means the pattern holds for species in all major groups that have diverged massively as the tree of life has grown throughout billions of years of evolution. Despite this rich diversity of life, our study shows basically all life forms remain remarkably constrained by this 'rule' on how temperature influences their ability to function. The best evolution has managed is to move this curve around -- life hasn't found a way to deviate from this one very specific thermal performance shape."

Searching for Exceptions to the Rule

The researchers now plan to use the UTPC as a reference point to investigate whether any organisms might slightly break away from this pattern.

"The next step is to use this model as something of a benchmark to see if there are any species or systems we can find that may, subtly, break away from this pattern. If we find any, we will be excited to ask why and how they do it -- especially given forecasts of how our climate is likely to keep warming in the next decades."
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific discovery of a universal temperature-performance curve that applies across all life forms, providing crucial insight into how species respond to temperature changes. This finding has broad implications for understanding biological limits in the face of climate change, which is a major global environmental challenge. The study is based on extensive data and offers a clear, focused advancement in knowledge that can help guide future research and conservation efforts.
articles.personality_title: Scientists find one temperature pattern that shapes all life on Earth
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists have long studied how temperature affects living things, but their findings were scattered in many different patterns for different species. Understanding these patterns is important because temperature influences how well animals, plants, and microbes can survive and grow.

**What happened** – Researchers at Trinity College Dublin discovered a universal thermal performance curve (UTPC) that applies to almost every kind of life. They studied over 2,500 performance curves from thousands of species, including bacteria, plants, reptiles, and insects. They found that all these curves follow the same basic shape: performance improves gradually as temperature rises until it reaches an optimum, then drops sharply when it gets too hot.

**Impact** – This discovery shows that despite the huge variety of life on Earth, all species share the same basic limits on how temperature affects their performance. It means evolution has not found a way to break free from this pattern. This is important because it suggests many species may struggle to adapt as the planet warms, limiting their ability to survive in hotter conditions.

**What's next step** – The scientists plan to use the UTPC as a standard to look for any species that might not follow this pattern. If they find such exceptions, they want to understand how those species manage to do so. This research could help predict which species are most at risk from climate change and guide conservation efforts.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A single temperature-performance curve governs all life, revealing shared limits on how species respond to warming and guiding future climate impact studies.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les scientifiques découvrent une courbe universelle de température qui régit toute vie sur Terre
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les scientifiques étudient depuis longtemps comment la température affecte les êtres vivants, mais leurs résultats étaient dispersés en plusieurs modèles différents selon les espèces. Comprendre ces modèles est important car la température influence la survie et la croissance des animaux, des plantes et des microbes.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs du Trinity College de Dublin ont découvert une courbe universelle de performance thermique (CUT) qui s’applique à presque toutes les formes de vie. Ils ont analysé plus de 2 500 courbes de performance provenant de milliers d’espèces, y compris des bactéries, des plantes, des reptiles et des insectes. Ils ont constaté que toutes ces courbes suivent la même forme de base : la performance s’améliore progressivement avec la hausse de la température jusqu’à un optimum, puis chute rapidement quand il fait trop chaud.

**Impact** – Cette découverte montre qu’en dépit de la grande diversité de la vie sur Terre, toutes les espèces partagent les mêmes limites de base sur l’effet de la température sur leur fonctionnement. Cela signifie que l’évolution n’a pas réussi à dépasser ce modèle. C’est important car cela suggère que de nombreuses espèces pourraient avoir du mal à s’adapter au réchauffement, limitant leur capacité à survivre dans des conditions plus chaudes.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques prévoient d’utiliser la CUT comme référence pour rechercher des espèces qui ne suivraient pas ce modèle. S’ils en trouvent, ils voudront comprendre comment ces espèces y parviennent. Cette recherche pourrait aider à prévoir quelles espèces sont les plus menacées par le changement climatique et à orienter les efforts de conservation.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Une seule courbe de performance thermique gouverne toute vie, révélant des limites communes à la réponse des espèces au réchauffement et guidant les futures études sur l’impact du climat.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos descubren un patrón universal de temperatura que rige toda la vida en la Tierra
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los científicos han estudiado durante mucho tiempo cómo la temperatura afecta a los seres vivos, pero sus resultados estaban dispersos en muchos patrones diferentes para distintas especies. Entender estos patrones es importante porque la temperatura influye en qué tan bien pueden sobrevivir y crecer animales, plantas y microbios.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores del Trinity College de Dublín descubrieron una curva universal de rendimiento térmico (CURT) que se aplica a casi todos los tipos de vida. Analizaron más de 2,500 curvas de rendimiento de miles de especies, incluyendo bacterias, plantas, reptiles e insectos. Encontraron que todas estas curvas siguen la misma forma básica: el rendimiento mejora gradualmente a medida que la temperatura sube hasta un punto óptimo, y luego cae rápidamente cuando hace demasiado calor.

**Impacto** – Este descubrimiento muestra que, a pesar de la gran variedad de vida en la Tierra, todas las especies comparten los mismos límites básicos sobre cómo la temperatura afecta su rendimiento. Esto significa que la evolución no ha encontrado una manera de romper este patrón. Es importante porque sugiere que muchas especies podrían tener dificultades para adaptarse al calentamiento global, limitando su capacidad para sobrevivir en condiciones más calientes.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos planean usar la CURT como referencia para buscar especies que puedan no seguir este patrón. Si encuentran algunas, quieren entender cómo lo logran. Esta investigación podría ayudar a predecir qué especies están más en riesgo por el cambio climático y guiar los esfuerzos de conservación.

**Resumen en una frase** – Una sola curva de rendimiento térmico rige toda la vida, revelando límites compartidos en la respuesta de las especies al calentamiento y guiando futuros estudios sobre el impacto del clima.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of an elegant, flowing thermal performance curve made of intertwining organic shapes representing diverse life forms—from bacteria to reptiles and fish—arranged along a smooth gradient of temperature colors, illustrating a shared pattern of growth and decline, set against a soft, natural background that evokes the interconnected tree of life without using literal trees or human figures.

articles.title: Cosmic rays turned ancient sand into a geological time machine
articles.summary: Scientists at Curtin University have uncovered a new way to read the deep history of Earth’s landscapes using microscopic zircon crystals from ancient beach sands. These incredibly durable minerals trap traces of krypton gas created when cosmic rays strike them at Earth’s surface, effectively turning each crystal into a “cosmic clock.” By measuring that krypton, researchers can determine how long sediments lingered near the surface before burial, revealing how landscapes eroded, shifted, and stabilized over millions of years.
articles.content:
Curtin University scientists have developed a new technique to explore the deep history of Australia's landscapes. The approach could help researchers understand how the environment responds to geological activity and climate shifts, while also offering clues about where valuable mineral deposits may be located.

The international research team was led by Curtin's Timescales of Mineral Systems Group at the School of Earth and Planetary Sciences, working with collaborators from the University of Göttingen and the University of Cologne. The scientists examined microscopic zircon crystals collected from ancient beach sands.

Zircon is among the most durable minerals found on Earth. Because it can withstand weathering, erosion and long journeys through rivers and coastlines, zircon grains can survive for millions of years while preserving information about their geological history.

Inside these zircon grains is a rare gas known as krypton. The gas forms when minerals near the Earth's surface are struck by cosmic rays (high-energy, charged subatomic particles from space).

By measuring the krypton trapped inside the crystals, the researchers were able to estimate how long the zircon grains remained near the surface before eventually being buried. This measurement acts like a "cosmic clock," allowing scientists to determine how quickly or slowly ancient landscapes eroded and shifted over extremely long periods.

A New Way to Study Ancient Landscapes

Lead author and Adjunct Curtin Research Fellow Dr. Maximilian Dröllner, who is also affiliated with the University of Göttingen, said the method makes it possible to investigate landscapes that are far older than scientists could previously analyze. The findings could help researchers better understand how the Earth's surface might respond to future climate changes and tectonic activity.

"Our planet's history shows climate and tectonic forces can control how landscapes behave over very long timescales," Dr. Dröllner said.

"This research helps us understand what happens when sea levels change and how deep-seated Earth movements influence the evolution of landscapes."

The study revealed that when landscapes remain tectonically stable and sea levels stay high, erosion slows significantly. Under those conditions, sediments can remain near the surface and be repeatedly reworked for millions of years.

Why These Findings Matter for the Future

Co-author and Timescales of Mineral Systems Group lead Professor Chris Kirkland said the results not only shed light on how Earth's surface has evolved over billions of years but may also inform future planning and land management.

"As we modify natural systems, we can expect changes in how sediment is stored in river basins and along coastlines and continental shelves," Professor Kirkland said.

"Our results show that these processes can fundamentally reshape landscapes, not just coastlines, over time."

Links Between Climate, Sediment, and Mineral Resources

Co-author Associate Professor Milo Barham, also part of the Timescales of Mineral Systems Group, noted that the research has important implications for understanding Australia's mineral resources.

"Climate doesn't just influence ecosystems and weather patterns, it also controls where mineral resources end up and how accessible they become," Associate Professor Barham said.

"Extended periods of sediment storage allow durable minerals to gradually concentrate while less stable materials break down, explaining why Australia hosts some of the world's most significant mineral sand deposits.

"Understanding these links is critical as demand for these minerals continues to grow, as it provides a long-term perspective that can improve models used to predict future environmental and resource outcomes arising from changes to these sediment systems."

The study, titled "Ancient landscape evolution tracked through cosmogenic krypton in detrital zircon," was published in PNAS.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific breakthrough where researchers developed a novel technique using cosmic-ray-produced krypton in zircon crystals to study ancient landscape evolution. This method provides meaningful insights into Earth's geological history, climate change impacts, and mineral resource distribution, offering broad benefits for environmental science, land management, and resource planning.
articles.personality_title: Scientists use cosmic rays to unlock Earth's ancient landscape history
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists want to understand how Earth's landscapes changed over millions of years. This helps us learn about climate shifts, geological activity, and where valuable minerals might be found.

**What happened** – Researchers at Curtin University and their partners studied tiny zircon crystals found in old beach sand. These crystals trap a rare gas called krypton, which forms when cosmic rays from space hit them near the Earth's surface. By measuring the krypton inside each crystal, scientists can tell how long the sand stayed near the surface before being buried.

**Impact** – This new technique acts like a “cosmic clock,” allowing scientists to track how landscapes eroded and changed over very long times. It can reveal how stable or active an area was and how sea level changes affected erosion. This helps us understand Earth's history better and may guide future land and resource management.

**What's next step** – Researchers plan to use this method to study other ancient landscapes and to improve predictions about how Earth's surface will respond to future climate and tectonic changes. It could also help locate important mineral deposits by showing how sediments move and concentrate over time.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Measuring cosmic-ray-produced krypton in tiny zircon crystals offers a powerful new way to read Earth's deep landscape history and better understand environmental change.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les scientifiques utilisent les rayons cosmiques pour révéler l'histoire ancienne des paysages terrestres
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les scientifiques cherchent à comprendre comment les paysages terrestres ont changé sur des millions d'années. Cela aide à mieux connaître les changements climatiques, l'activité géologique et où trouver des minéraux précieux.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Des chercheurs de l'université Curtin et leurs partenaires ont étudié de minuscules cristaux de zircon dans du sable de plage ancien. Ces cristaux piègent un gaz rare appelé krypton, formé lorsque des rayons cosmiques venus de l'espace les frappent près de la surface de la Terre. En mesurant le krypton dans chaque cristal, les scientifiques peuvent savoir combien de temps le sable est resté près de la surface avant d'être enterré.

**Impact** – Cette nouvelle technique fonctionne comme une « horloge cosmique », permettant de suivre l’érosion et les changements des paysages sur de très longues périodes. Elle révèle la stabilité ou l’activité d’une région et comment les variations du niveau de la mer ont influencé l’érosion. Cela améliore notre compréhension de l’histoire de la Terre et peut guider la gestion future des terres et des ressources.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs vont appliquer cette méthode à d’autres paysages anciens et améliorer les prévisions sur la façon dont la surface terrestre réagira aux futurs changements climatiques et tectoniques. Cela pourrait aussi aider à localiser des gisements miniers importants en montrant comment les sédiments se déplacent et se concentrent avec le temps.

**Phrase clé** – Mesurer le krypton produit par les rayons cosmiques dans de petits cristaux de zircon offre un nouveau moyen puissant de lire l’histoire profonde des paysages terrestres et de mieux comprendre les changements environnementaux.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos usan rayos cósmicos para descubrir la historia antigua de los paisajes terrestres
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los científicos quieren entender cómo han cambiado los paisajes de la Tierra durante millones de años. Esto ayuda a conocer mejor los cambios climáticos, la actividad geológica y dónde encontrar minerales valiosos.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores de la Universidad Curtin y sus socios estudiaron pequeños cristales de circón en arena de playa antigua. Estos cristales atrapan un gas raro llamado criptón, que se forma cuando los rayos cósmicos del espacio los golpean cerca de la superficie de la Tierra. Midiendo el criptón dentro de cada cristal, los científicos pueden saber cuánto tiempo la arena permaneció cerca de la superficie antes de ser enterrada.

**Impacto** – Esta nueva técnica funciona como un “reloj cósmico”, permitiendo seguir cómo la erosión y los paisajes cambiaron durante mucho tiempo. Revela si un área fue estable o activa y cómo los cambios en el nivel del mar afectaron la erosión. Esto mejora nuestra comprensión de la historia de la Tierra y puede ayudar a manejar mejor las tierras y recursos en el futuro.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores planean usar este método para estudiar otros paisajes antiguos y mejorar predicciones sobre cómo la superficie de la Tierra responderá a futuros cambios climáticos y tectónicos. También podría ayudar a localizar depósitos minerales importantes mostrando cómo se mueven y concentran los sedimentos con el tiempo.

**Frase clave** – Medir el criptón producido por rayos cósmicos en pequeños cristales de circón ofrece una nueva forma poderosa de leer la historia profunda de los paisajes terrestres y entender mejor los cambios ambientales.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of ancient beach sands scattered with glowing zircon crystals, each subtly illuminated from within by tiny cosmic rays represented as gentle streams of light from a starry sky above, set against a softly layered landscape showing shifting coastlines and geological strata beneath, rendered in natural earth tones and soft blues.

articles.title: Scientists discover hedgehogs can hear ultrasound and it could save them from cars
articles.summary: Researchers have discovered that hedgehogs can hear ultrasound, a surprising ability that could help protect them from cars. Since road traffic kills large numbers of hedgehogs, scientists believe ultrasonic repellents might be used to steer them away from danger. The animals’ ears appear specially adapted for detecting high-frequency sounds. If the idea works, cars could one day emit signals that warn hedgehogs before it’s too late.
articles.content:
Scientists at the University of Oxford say ultrasonic sound devices might one day help reduce the number of hedgehogs killed by cars. Their idea stems from new research published March 11 in Biology Letters showing for the first time that hedgehogs are capable of hearing high-frequency ultrasound.

The European hedgehog is one of Europe's most familiar wild mammals, yet its numbers have been falling sharply. In 2024, the International Union for Conservation of Nature reclassified the species as "near threatened." Road traffic is a major contributor to these losses, with vehicle collisions estimated to kill as many as one in three hedgehogs in some local populations.

The new findings suggest that ultrasonic repellents might be used to discourage hedgehogs from entering roads and other dangerous areas. Until now, researchers did not know whether hedgehogs were able to hear sounds in this frequency range.

Lead researcher Assistant Professor Sophie Lund Rasmussen (Wildlife Conservation Research Unit/Department of Biology, University of Oxford and University of Copenhagen) said: "Having discovered that hedgehogs can hear in ultrasound, the next stage will be to find collaborators within the car industry to fund and design sound repellents for cars. If our future research shows that it proves possible to design an effective device to keep hedgehogs away from cars, this could have a significant impact in reducing the threat of road traffic to the declining European hedgehog."

Testing Hedgehog Hearing

To investigate hedgehog hearing, researchers at the University of Oxford worked with collaborators in Denmark to measure the auditory brainstem response of 20 rehabilitated hedgehogs from Danish wildlife rescue centers. This technique records electrical activity between the inner ear and the brain using small electrodes placed on the animals while short bursts of sound are played through a small loudspeaker.

The measurements showed that the hedgehogs' brainstems responded to sounds ranging from 4-85 kHz, with their strongest sensitivity around 40 kHz. This confirms that hedgehogs can detect ultrasonic sounds (which starts at frequencies greater than 20 kHz) and can hear frequencies up to at least 85 kHz. After the experiments, a veterinarian checked the animals and they were returned to the wild the following night.

Ear Structure Built for High Frequency Sound

The researchers also used high-resolution micro CT scans to examine the ear of a deceased hedgehog (which had been euthanized after being critically injured by a rat trap). Using these scans, the team created a detailed interactive 3D model of the hedgehog ear that revealed previously unknown features.

The model showed that hedgehogs possess very small, dense middle ear bones along with a partially fused joint between the eardrum and the first of these bones. This arrangement stiffens the chain of bones and allows it to transmit very high-pitched sounds more efficiently, a characteristic often seen in animals such as echolocating bats that can detect ultrasound.

The scans also showed that hedgehogs have a small stapes (the smallest middle-ear bone that connects the chain of ear bones to the inner ear's fluid-filled cochlea). Because a smaller and lighter stapes can vibrate more rapidly, it can transfer high-frequency sound waves more effectively. Researchers also found that the cochlea itself is relatively short and compact, which helps it process ultrasonic vibrations.

Ultrasonic Repellers Could Reduce Hedgehog Deaths

Taken together, these findings indicate that ultrasonic repellents could potentially be designed so hedgehogs can hear them while humans and pets cannot.* If future research confirms that such devices are effective, they could be used to keep hedgehogs away from roads as well as other dangers like robotic lawnmowers and garden strimmers.

Dr. Rasmussen added, "Our novel results revealed that European hedgehogs are designed to, and can, perceive a broad ultrasonic range. A fascinating question now is whether they use ultrasound to communicate with each other, or to detect prey - something we have already begun investigating."

Co-author Professor David Macdonald (Wildlife Conservation Research Unit/Department of Biology, University of Oxford) said: "It is especially exciting when research motivated by conservation leads to a fundamental new discovery about a species biology which, full circle, in turn offers a new avenue for conservation. The critical question now is whether the hedgehogs respond to ultrasound in ways that might reduce the risks of collisions with robotic lawnmowers or even cars."

The research involved scientists from the University of Oxford, The Natural History Museum Denmark at University of Copenhagen, City Dyreklinik (Copenhagen), Aarhus University, Aarhus University Hospital, and the University of Southern Denmark.

* Humans hear in the range of 20-20,000 Hz, dogs 67- 45,000 Hz, and cats 45-65,000 Hz.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific discovery that hedgehogs can hear ultrasound, which opens the possibility of developing ultrasonic repellents to reduce roadkill and protect a declining species. This has broad environmental and conservation implications for European hedgehogs, a species now near threatened, and presents a tangible, innovative solution with potential large-scale impact on wildlife preservation.
articles.personality_title: Scientists find hedgehogs can hear ultrasound, offering new way to protect them from cars
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – European hedgehogs are facing serious threats from cars, with many killed on roads. Their numbers have dropped enough that they are now considered 'near threatened' by conservation groups.

**What happened** – Researchers from the University of Oxford and partners discovered that hedgehogs can hear very high-pitched sounds called ultrasound. They tested 20 rescued hedgehogs using special equipment that measures brain responses to sounds. The hedgehogs responded to sounds up to 85 kHz, well beyond what humans can hear. Scientists also studied their ear bones and found they are specially built to detect these high-frequency sounds.

**Impact** – This discovery is important because it means new devices using ultrasound could be made to warn hedgehogs away from roads before cars arrive. Such ultrasonic repellents would be silent to people and pets but noticeable to hedgehogs, potentially saving many lives. It also opens questions about how hedgehogs might use ultrasound naturally.

**What's next step** – Researchers want to work with car makers to develop and test ultrasonic devices that keep hedgehogs safe. They will also study if hedgehogs use ultrasound to communicate or find food. If successful, this could reduce deaths from cars and other dangers like lawnmowers.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Finding that hedgehogs can hear ultrasound offers a new way to protect them from road accidents using sound devices humans cannot hear.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les scientifiques découvrent que les hérissons entendent les ultrasons, une aide pour les protéger des voitures
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les hérissons européens sont gravement menacés par les voitures, beaucoup étant tués sur les routes. Leur nombre a suffisamment baissé pour qu'ils soient désormais considérés comme « quasi menacés » par les groupes de conservation.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs de l’Université d’Oxford et de leurs partenaires ont découvert que les hérissons peuvent entendre des sons très aigus appelés ultrasons. Ils ont testé 20 hérissons sauvés en utilisant un équipement spécial mesurant la réponse cérébrale aux sons. Les hérissons réagissaient à des sons allant jusqu’à 85 kHz, bien au-delà de ce que les humains entendent. Les scientifiques ont aussi étudié leurs osselets de l’oreille et trouvé qu’ils sont spécialement conçus pour détecter ces sons à haute fréquence.

**Impact** – Cette découverte est importante car elle signifie que de nouveaux appareils utilisant les ultrasons pourraient être créés pour avertir les hérissons avant l’arrivée des voitures. Ces répulsifs ultrasoniques seraient inaudibles pour les humains et les animaux domestiques, mais perceptibles par les hérissons, ce qui pourrait sauver de nombreuses vies. Cela soulève aussi des questions sur l’utilisation naturelle des ultrasons par les hérissons.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs veulent collaborer avec des constructeurs automobiles pour développer et tester des dispositifs ultrasoniques protégeant les hérissons. Ils étudieront aussi si les hérissons utilisent les ultrasons pour communiquer ou chercher leur nourriture. Si cela fonctionne, cela pourrait réduire les décès causés par les voitures et d’autres dangers comme les tondeuses robotisées.

**Résumé en une phrase** – La découverte que les hérissons entendent les ultrasons offre une nouvelle méthode pour les protéger des accidents de la route grâce à des dispositifs sonores inaudibles pour les humains.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos descubren que los erizos oyen ultrasonidos, lo que podría protegerlos de los coches
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los erizos europeos están en peligro debido a los coches, que matan a muchos en las carreteras. Su número ha bajado tanto que ahora se les considera «casi amenazados» por grupos de conservación.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores de la Universidad de Oxford y colaboradores descubrieron que los erizos pueden oír sonidos de alta frecuencia llamados ultrasonidos. Probaron a 20 erizos rescatados usando un equipo especial que mide la respuesta del cerebro a los sonidos. Los erizos respondieron a sonidos de hasta 85 kHz, mucho más allá de lo que los humanos pueden oír. También estudiaron sus huesos del oído y encontraron que están diseñados para detectar estos sonidos agudos.

**Impacto** – Este hallazgo es importante porque significa que podrían crearse dispositivos que emitan ultrasonidos para advertir a los erizos antes de que pasen los coches. Estos repelentes serían silenciosos para humanos y mascotas, pero perceptibles para los erizos, lo que podría salvar muchas vidas. También abre preguntas sobre si los erizos usan ultrasonidos para comunicarse o cazar.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores quieren colaborar con fabricantes de coches para desarrollar y probar dispositivos ultrasonicos que protejan a los erizos. También estudiarán si los erizos usan ultrasonidos para comunicarse o encontrar alimento. Si funciona, podría reducir muertes por coches y otros peligros como cortadoras de césped robotizadas.

**Resumen en una frase** – El descubrimiento de que los erizos oyen ultrasonidos ofrece una forma nueva de protegerlos de accidentes de tráfico con dispositivos sonoros que los humanos no pueden oír.
articles.image_prompt: A gentle, detailed painting of a European hedgehog with its ears slightly enlarged and stylized to show delicate, intricate bone structures, surrounded by soft, visible sound waves in high-frequency patterns gently radiating outward, set against a calm rural roadside at dusk with a softly glowing car silhouette in the distance, all rendered in warm, natural earth tones.

articles.title: OBSERVER: Artificial Intelligence and Earth Observation workshop looks into the future of EO in Europe
articles.summary:
OBSERVER: Artificial Intelligence and Earth Observation workshop looks into the future of EO in Europe 
sonia
Thu, 12/03/2026 - 13:39

Artificial Intelligence is transforming how Earth Observation data are analysed and used to support environmental monitoring, climate research and action, as well as disaster risk reduction and emergency management. AI methods, including machine learning techniques, are helping researchers, public institutions, and industry extract insights more quickly from increasingly large volumes of satellite data. To explore these developments, policymakers, scientists, and industry representatives gathered in Brussels on 9–10 March 2026 for the workshop Artificial Intelligence and Earth Observation: From Innovation to Services. Discussions examined how AI is reshaping Copernicus and the Destination Earth (DestinE) initiative, supporting the evolution of operational EO services and modelling capabilities. In this Observer, we look at some highlights from the workshop. 


The workshop explored how the growing nexus between Artificial Intelligence and Earth Observation represents a key opportunity for Copernicus and its implementing entities. Credit: European Commission. 

 
Artificial Intelligence (AI) is reshaping how Earth Observation (EO) data are analysed and used to support services for environmental monitoring, climate research, and disaster response. These developments were the focus of the workshop Artificial Intelligence and Earth Observation: From Innovation to Services. Held in Brussels on 9–10 March 2026 and organised by the European Commission, the event featured high-level keynote presentations, technical briefings, panel discussions, and hands-on demonstrations, attracting more than 1,500 registrants, including around 200 who attended in person. 
Opening the event, representatives from the Commission emphasised that AI has become a “game changer” for EO. As satellite missions and environmental monitoring systems generate ever larger volumes of data, AI techniques are increasingly enabling to extract insights quickly and integrate them into existing operational services. 


Thomas Skordas, Deputy Director-General for Communications Networks, Content and Technology, described artificial intelligence as a “game changer” for Earth Observation. Credit: European Commission. 

 
Discussions throughout the two days highlighted Europe’s ambition to become the world’s “AI continent”, combining regulatory leadership with advanced and powerful digital infrastructure, high-quality datasets, and a dynamic innovation ecosystem. In this context, Copernicus and the Destination Earth (DestinE) initiative were repeatedly described as key pillars of Europe’s strategy. Copernicus provides one of the largest collections of open EO data globally, while DestinE is developing digital twins of the Earth system combining satellite observations, modelling, and AI. 
Together, these initiatives create the environment in which AI can be increasingly integrated into the EO value chain, helping transform raw satellite data into operational knowledge for science, policy, and society. 
 
Turning satellite data into operational insights 
Machine learning techniques are allowing researchers and service providers to process large volumes of satellite observations more efficiently and identify patterns which would otherwise remain difficult to detect. During the workshop, the European Centre for Medium-Range Weather Forecasts (ECMWF) presented examples of how machine learning models trained using datasets such as Copernicus Climate Change Service’s ERA5 can improve environmental forecasting. ERA5, one of the most widely used climate reanalysis datasets, provides detailed historical records of atmospheric conditions and has become an important resource for developing AI-based weather prediction models. By combining these data-driven approaches with established physical models, researchers are developing new tools capable of improving predictions of environmental variables and supporting earlier warnings of extreme weather events. 


Forecast in a box, introduced during the workshop, allows users to customise the information and plots they want to generate and run an AI-based forecasting model independently. Credit: European Commission. 

 
AI is also enabling new ways for users to interact with EO data. Emerging tools based on conversational interfaces and intelligent assistants can help users navigate complex datasets more easily, identify relevant information, and generate customised analyses. One example is Copernicus Observia AI, an AI assistant built on the European Environment Agency's (EEA) GPT-LAB platform which guides users through finding, exploring and using Copernicus Land Monitoring Service data, while ensuring responses are grounded in trusted Copernicus sources. 
 
Infrastructure powering the AI revolution 
While AI provides powerful analytical capabilities, its development depends on a robust digital infrastructure. A recurring theme throughout the workshop was the importance of combining high-quality data, efficient workflows, and advanced computing resources to support AI applications in EO. 
Tiago Quintino, Head of Development Section at ECMWF, emphasised the importance of high-quality data for AI, noting that “data is the new oil” and that large volumes of reliable observations are essential for producing robust AI outputs. Copernicus was frequently highlighted as a key advantage for Europe in this context. Its satellite missions and open and free data policy provide large volumes of high-quality time series which can be used to train and validate machine learning models. 
At the same time, the importance of expanding Europe’s computing capacity was also underlined. Initiatives such as EuroHPC are helping to strengthen the continent’s high-performance computing capabilities, while new AI factories and future AI “gigafactories” aim to significantly increase computing power available for AI development. Together, these investments are helping create an ecosystem in which EO data, AI, and advanced computing infrastructure can be combined to support the next generation of environmental monitoring and forecasting applications. 
 
Digital twins open new possibilities for environmental modelling 
The workshop also highlighted the growing role of digital twins, particularly within DestinE. These digital representations combine EO data with advanced modelling frameworks to simulate environmental processes and explore future, “what if”, scenarios. 


Using high-performance computing, machine learning, and satellite data, the digital twins of Destination Earth (DestinE) combine Earth Observation data with numerical models and AI techniques to simulate the behaviour of the Earth system and explore possible future scenarios. Credit: ESA. 

 
Examples demonstrated how digital twins will support decision-making in areas such as ocean management. By combining satellite observations, modelling systems, and machine learning techniques, these platforms can improve predictions and allow researchers to explore how environmental systems may respond to different conditions. 
Sessions also highlighted the potential efficiency gains delivered by AI-based approaches. Machine learning models can sometimes generate results comparable to those of traditional simulations while requiring significantly less computing power, allowing more frequent or interactive analyses. 
 
Building trustworthy AI for the future of EO 
Across both days, speakers emphasised the importance of developing trustworthy AI systems. In scientific and operational contexts, maintaining confidence in AI-powered insights requires transparency, reproducibility, and traceability back to the underlying datasets. 


AI4FloodDamage demonstration with the Head of Copernicus Emergency Management Service, Peter Salamon. Credit: European Commission. 

 
Speakers stressed that AI should complement rather than replace the numerical models based on physical laws used in Earth system science. By combining data-driven approaches with established scientific methods, researchers can develop more robust tools for analysing environmental change. 
Transparency also emerged as an important issue. Users often want to understand how AI models generate their results, particularly when these outputs inform important decisions. Ensuring that AI systems are explainable and properly validated is therefore essential for building trust in AI-enabled EO services. 
As discussions during the workshop made clear, Europe’s strategy for the future of EO relies on combining high-quality satellite data, advanced modelling capabilities, powerful computing infrastructure, and trustworthy AI.  

Thu, 12/03/2026 - 12:00
articles.content:
OBSERVER: Artificial Intelligence and Earth Observation workshop looks into the future of EO in Europe

Thu, 12/03/2026 - 12:00

Artificial Intelligence is transforming how Earth Observation data are analysed and used to support environmental monitoring, climate research and action, as well as disaster risk reduction and emergency management. AI methods, including machine learning techniques, are helping researchers, public institutions, and industry extract insights more quickly from increasingly large volumes of satellite data. To explore these developments, policymakers, scientists, and industry representatives gathered in Brussels on 9–10 March 2026 for the workshop Artificial Intelligence and Earth Observation: From Innovation to Services. Discussions examined how AI is reshaping Copernicus and the Destination Earth (DestinE) initiative, supporting the evolution of operational EO services and modelling capabilities. In this Observer, we look at some highlights from the workshop.

The workshop explored how the growing nexus between Artificial Intelligence and Earth Observation represents a key opportunity for Copernicus and its implementing entities. Credit: European Commission.

Artificial Intelligence (AI) is reshaping how Earth Observation (EO) data are analysed and used to support services for environmental monitoring, climate research, and disaster response. These developments were the focus of the workshop Artificial Intelligence and Earth Observation: From Innovation to Services. Held in Brussels on 9–10 March 2026 and organised by the European Commission, the event featured high-level keynote presentations, technical briefings, panel discussions, and hands-on demonstrations, attracting more than 1,500 registrants, including around 200 who attended in person.

Opening the event, representatives from the Commission emphasised that AI has become a “game changer” for EO. As satellite missions and environmental monitoring systems generate ever larger volumes of data, AI techniques are increasingly enabling to extract insights quickly and integrate them into existing operational services.

Thomas Skordas, Deputy Director-General for Communications Networks, Content and Technology, described artificial intelligence as a “game changer” for Earth Observation. Credit: European Commission.

Discussions throughout the two days highlighted Europe’s ambition to become the world’s “AI continent”, combining regulatory leadership with advanced and powerful digital infrastructure, high-quality datasets, and a dynamic innovation ecosystem. In this context, Copernicus and the Destination Earth (DestinE) initiative were repeatedly described as key pillars of Europe’s strategy. Copernicus provides one of the largest collections of open EO data globally, while DestinE is developing digital twins of the Earth system combining satellite observations, modelling, and AI.

Together, these initiatives create the environment in which AI can be increasingly integrated into the EO value chain, helping transform raw satellite data into operational knowledge for science, policy, and society.

Turning satellite data into operational insights

Machine learning techniques are allowing researchers and service providers to process large volumes of satellite observations more efficiently and identify patterns which would otherwise remain difficult to detect. During the workshop, the European Centre for Medium-Range Weather Forecasts (ECMWF) presented examples of how machine learning models trained using datasets such as Copernicus Climate Change Service’s ERA5 can improve environmental forecasting. ERA5 , one of the most widely used climate reanalysis datasets, provides detailed historical records of atmospheric conditions and has become an important resource for developing AI-based weather prediction models. By combining these data-driven approaches with established physical models, researchers are developing new tools capable of improving predictions of environmental variables and supporting earlier warnings of extreme weather events.

Forecast in a box, introduced during the workshop, allows users to customise the information and plots they want to generate and run an AI-based forecasting model independently. Credit: European Commission.

AI is also enabling new ways for users to interact with EO data. Emerging tools based on conversational interfaces and intelligent assistants can help users navigate complex datasets more easily, identify relevant information, and generate customised analyses. One example is Copernicus Observia AI , an AI assistant built on the European Environment Agency's (EEA) GPT-LAB platform which guides users through finding, exploring and using Copernicus Land Monitoring Service data, while ensuring responses are grounded in trusted Copernicus sources.

Infrastructure powering the AI revolution

While AI provides powerful analytical capabilities, its development depends on a robust digital infrastructure. A recurring theme throughout the workshop was the importance of combining high-quality data, efficient workflows, and advanced computing resources to support AI applications in EO.

Tiago Quintino, Head of Development Section at ECMWF, emphasised the importance of high-quality data for AI, noting that “data is the new oil” and that large volumes of reliable observations are essential for producing robust AI outputs. Copernicus was frequently highlighted as a key advantage for Europe in this context. Its satellite missions and open and free data policy provide large volumes of high-quality time series which can be used to train and validate machine learning models.

At the same time, the importance of expanding Europe’s computing capacity was also underlined. Initiatives such as EuroHPC are helping to strengthen the continent’s high-performance computing capabilities, while new AI factories and future AI “gigafactories” aim to significantly increase computing power available for AI development. Together, these investments are helping create an ecosystem in which EO data, AI, and advanced computing infrastructure can be combined to support the next generation of environmental monitoring and forecasting applications.

Digital twins open new possibilities for environmental modelling

The workshop also highlighted the growing role of digital twins, particularly within DestinE. These digital representations combine EO data with advanced modelling frameworks to simulate environmental processes and explore future, “what if”, scenarios.

Using high-performance computing, machine learning, and satellite data, the digital twins of Destination Earth (DestinE) combine Earth Observation data with numerical models and AI techniques to simulate the behaviour of the Earth system and explore possible future scenarios. Credit: ESA.

Examples demonstrated how digital twins will support decision-making in areas such as ocean management. By combining satellite observations, modelling systems, and machine learning techniques, these platforms can improve predictions and allow researchers to explore how environmental systems may respond to different conditions.

Sessions also highlighted the potential efficiency gains delivered by AI-based approaches. Machine learning models can sometimes generate results comparable to those of traditional simulations while requiring significantly less computing power, allowing more frequent or interactive analyses.

Building trustworthy AI for the future of EO

Across both days, speakers emphasised the importance of developing trustworthy AI systems. In scientific and operational contexts, maintaining confidence in AI-powered insights requires transparency, reproducibility, and traceability back to the underlying datasets.

AI4FloodDamage demonstration with the Head of Copernicus Emergency Management Service, Peter Salamon. Credit: European Commission.

Speakers stressed that AI should complement rather than replace the numerical models based on physical laws used in Earth system science. By combining data-driven approaches with established scientific methods, researchers can develop more robust tools for analysing environmental change.

Transparency also emerged as an important issue. Users often want to understand how AI models generate their results, particularly when these outputs inform important decisions. Ensuring that AI systems are explainable and properly validated is therefore essential for building trust in AI-enabled EO services.

As discussions during the workshop made clear, Europe’s strategy for the future of EO relies on combining high-quality satellite data, advanced modelling capabilities, powerful computing infrastructure, and trustworthy AI.
articles.reasoning: The article reports on a significant workshop highlighting how AI is transforming Earth Observation data analysis to support environmental monitoring, climate research, disaster risk reduction, and emergency management. It describes Europe's strategic initiatives like Copernicus and Destination Earth that leverage AI, satellite data, and advanced computing to create operational insights and digital twins for environmental modeling. The story has broad significance for society and the environment, with detailed context on technological and policy advances.
articles.personality_title: Europe uses AI and satellites to better understand and protect the Earth
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Earth Observation (EO) means collecting data about our planet using satellites. This information helps monitor the environment, study climate change, and prepare for disasters. Recently, artificial intelligence (AI) has started to change how this data is analyzed, making it faster and smarter.

**What happened** – On March 9–10, 2026, more than 1,500 people, including scientists, policymakers, and industry experts, met in Brussels for a workshop called “Artificial Intelligence and Earth Observation: From Innovation to Services.” They discussed how AI is improving Europe’s EO programs, especially two big projects: Copernicus, which shares satellite data openly, and Destination Earth (DestinE), which creates digital models of Earth to predict future changes.

**Impact** – AI helps turn huge amounts of satellite data into useful information quickly. For example, machine learning improves weather forecasts by analyzing past climate data combined with physical models. New AI tools also allow people to explore environmental data more easily using smart assistants. Digital twins, detailed computer models of Earth, help researchers test “what if” scenarios, like how oceans might change. Europe’s strong satellite data, better computing power, and careful AI design make these advances possible.

**What’s next step** – Europe plans to keep building powerful computers and AI systems to improve EO services. They will focus on making AI trustworthy by ensuring it explains how it works and supports, not replaces, traditional scientific methods. These efforts aim to provide better tools for environmental decision-making, disaster preparedness, and climate action.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Europe is combining AI, satellite data, and advanced computing to create smarter tools that help understand and protect our planet.
articles.personality_title_fr: L’Europe utilise l’IA et les satellites pour mieux comprendre et protéger la Terre
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – L’observation de la Terre (OT) consiste à collecter des données sur notre planète grâce aux satellites. Ces informations aident à surveiller l’environnement, étudier le changement climatique et se préparer aux catastrophes. Récemment, l’intelligence artificielle (IA) a commencé à transformer l’analyse de ces données, la rendant plus rapide et plus intelligente.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Les 9 et 10 mars 2026, plus de 1 500 personnes, dont des scientifiques, décideurs et experts industriels, se sont réunies à Bruxelles lors de l’atelier « Intelligence artificielle et observation de la Terre : de l’innovation aux services ». Ils ont discuté de la manière dont l’IA améliore les programmes européens d’OT, notamment deux grands projets : Copernicus, qui partage librement les données satellites, et Destination Terre (DestinE), qui crée des modèles numériques de la Terre pour prévoir les changements futurs.

**Impact** – L’IA permet de transformer rapidement d’énormes volumes de données satellites en informations utiles. Par exemple, l’apprentissage automatique améliore les prévisions météorologiques en analysant les données climatiques passées combinées à des modèles physiques. De nouveaux outils d’IA permettent aussi aux utilisateurs d’explorer plus facilement les données environnementales grâce à des assistants intelligents. Les jumeaux numériques, modèles informatiques détaillés de la Terre, aident les chercheurs à tester des scénarios « et si », comme l’évolution des océans. Les données satellites solides de l’Europe, une meilleure puissance de calcul et une conception rigoureuse de l’IA rendent ces progrès possibles.

**Prochaine étape** – L’Europe prévoit de continuer à développer des ordinateurs puissants et des systèmes d’IA pour améliorer les services d’OT. Elle mettra l’accent sur la confiance dans l’IA en garantissant qu’elle explique son fonctionnement et soutient, sans remplacer, les méthodes scientifiques traditionnelles. Ces efforts visent à fournir de meilleurs outils pour la prise de décision environnementale, la préparation aux catastrophes et l’action climatique.

**Résumé en une phrase** – L’Europe combine l’IA, les données satellites et la puissance de calcul pour créer des outils plus intelligents qui aident à comprendre et protéger notre planète.
articles.personality_title_es: Europa usa IA y satélites para entender y proteger mejor la Tierra
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La Observación de la Tierra (OT) consiste en recopilar datos sobre nuestro planeta mediante satélites. Esta información ayuda a monitorear el medio ambiente, estudiar el cambio climático y prepararse para desastres. Recientemente, la inteligencia artificial (IA) ha comenzado a cambiar la forma en que se analizan estos datos, haciéndolo más rápido e inteligente.

**Qué pasó** – El 9 y 10 de marzo de 2026, más de 1,500 personas, incluyendo científicos, responsables políticos y expertos de la industria, se reunieron en Bruselas en el taller “Inteligencia Artificial y Observación de la Tierra: De la Innovación a los Servicios.” Discutieron cómo la IA está mejorando los programas europeos de OT, especialmente dos grandes proyectos: Copernicus, que comparte datos satelitales abiertamente, y Destination Earth (DestinE), que crea modelos digitales de la Tierra para predecir cambios futuros.

**Impacto** – La IA ayuda a convertir grandes cantidades de datos satelitales en información útil rápidamente. Por ejemplo, el aprendizaje automático mejora las predicciones meteorológicas analizando datos climáticos pasados junto con modelos físicos. Nuevas herramientas de IA permiten a las personas explorar datos ambientales más fácilmente usando asistentes inteligentes. Los gemelos digitales, modelos informáticos detallados de la Tierra, ayudan a los investigadores a probar escenarios de “qué pasaría si”, como cómo podrían cambiar los océanos. Los sólidos datos satelitales de Europa, su mejor capacidad informática y el diseño cuidadoso de la IA hacen posibles estos avances.

**Próximo paso** – Europa planea seguir construyendo computadoras potentes y sistemas de IA para mejorar los servicios de OT. Se enfocarán en hacer que la IA sea confiable asegurando que explique cómo funciona y que apoye, no reemplace, los métodos científicos tradicionales. Estos esfuerzos buscan proporcionar mejores herramientas para la toma de decisiones ambientales, la preparación ante desastres y la acción climática.

**Resumen en una frase** – Europa combina IA, datos satelitales y computación avanzada para crear herramientas más inteligentes que ayudan a entender y proteger nuestro planeta.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of an abstract globe composed of interconnected satellite dishes and digital data streams flowing into a glowing, transparent Earth-shaped crystal, surrounded by softly lit, stylized AI neural network patterns and ethereal cloud-like formations symbolizing environmental monitoring and climate forecasting, rendered in natural, muted blues, greens, and earth tones.
